analogy: Think of "Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement" through the chapter's own opening images: THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 286 Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be y y y y yond ond ond ond ond Fig. 11.1... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 286 Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be Civil Disobedience and Be y y y y yond ond ond ond ond Fig.

The text explains that 11.1 People gather on the banks of the Sabarmati River to hear Mahatma Gandhi speak before starting out on the Salt March in 1930 In the history of nationalism a single individual is often identified with the making of a nation.

The text explains that thus, for example, we associate Garibaldi with the making of Italy, George Washington with the American War of Independence, and Ho Chi Minh with the struggle to free Vietnam from colonial rule.

The text explains that in the same manner, Mahatma Gandhi has been regarded as the ‘Father’ of the Indian nation.

The text explains that in so far as Gandhiji was the most influential and revered of all the leaders who participated in the freedom struggle, that characterisation is not misplaced.

The text explains that however, like Washington or Ho Chi-Minh, Mahatma Gandhi’s political career was shaped and constrained by the society in which he lived.

The text explains that for individuals, even great ones, are made by history even as they make history.

The text explains that this chapter analyses Gandhiji’s activities in India during the crucial period 1915-1948.

The text explains that it explores his interactions with different sections of the Indian society and the popular struggles that he inspired and led.

The text explains that it introduces the student to the different kinds of sources that historians use in reconstructing the career of a leader and of the social movements that he was associated with.

The text explains that a Leader Announces Himself In January 1915, Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi returned to his homeland after two decades of residence abroad.

The text explains that these years had been spent for the most part in South Africa, where he went as a lawyer, and in time became a leader of the Indian community in that territory.

The text explains that as the historian Chandran Devanesan has remarked, South Africa was “the making of the Mahatma”.

The text explains that it was in South Africa that Mahatma Gandhi first forged the distinctive techniques of non-violent protest known as satyagraha, first promoted harmony between religions, and first alerted upper -caste Indians to their discriminatory treatment of low castes and women.

The text explains that the India that Mahatma Gandhi came back to in 1915 was rather different from the one that he had left in 1893.

The text explains that although still a colony of the British, it was far more active in a political sense.

The text explains that source 1 Mahatma Gandhi was profoundly critical of the modern age in which machines enslaved humans and displaced labour.

The text explains that where most Source 2 MAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT The miraculous and the unbelievable contd

The text explains that from various sources I hear tales of such wanton destruction of the nation’s property in all parts of India.

The text explains that 49 Source 4 Ü What does the speech tell us about how Gandhiji saw the colonial state?

The text explains that he is gathering more strength as he proceeds.” The progress of the Salt March can also be traced from another source: the American newsmagazine, Time.

The text explains that 312 Source 6 MAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT

The text explains that through 1940 and 1941, the Congress organised a series of individual satyagrahas to pressure the rulers to promise freedom once the war had ended.

The text explains that meanwhile, back in India, the Viceroy, Lord Wavell, brought the Congress and the League together for a series of talks.

The text explains that after the talks broke down, Jinnah called for a “Direct Action Day” to press the League’s demand for Pakistan.

The text explains that in February 1947, Wavell was replaced as Viceroy by Lord Mountbatten.

The text explains that knowing Gandhi There are many different kinds of sources from which we can reconstruct the political career of Gandhiji and the history of the nationalist movement.

The text explains that 6.1 Public voice and private scripts One important source is the writings and speeches of Mahatma Gandhi and his contemporaries, including both his associates and his political adversaries.

The text explains that source 7 MAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT One event through letters In the 1920s, Jawaharlal Nehru was increasingly influenced by socialism, and he returned from Europe in 1928 deeply impressed with the Soviet Union.

The text explains that there is regular continuous campaign against us treating us as persons whose time is over, who represent and stand for ideas that are worn out and that have no present value, who are only obstructing the progress of the country and who deserve to be cast out of the positions which they undeservedly hold … we have felt that a great injustice has been and is being done to us by others, and we are not receiving the protection we are entitled from you as our colleague and as our President … Yours sincerely Rajendra Prasad Source 7 (contd) Wardha, July 1, 1936 From A Bunch of Old Letters

The text explains that mAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT Source 7 (contd)

The text explains that 6.3 Through police eyes Another vital source is government records, for the colonial rulers kept close tabs on those they regarded as critical of the government.

The text explains that let us look at one such source: the fortnightly reports that were prepared by the Home Department from the early twentieth century.

The text explains that to what extent and in what directions they will affect political condition in this province, it is difficult to surmise at present.

The text explains that mAHATMA GANDHI AND THE NATIONALIST MOVEMENT Source 8 contd Fortnightly Reports of the Home Department (Confidential)

The text explains that he was at once taken direct to Naini Central Jail, where he was tried and sentenced to six months simple imprisonment.

The text explains that 313 6.4 From newspapers One more important source is contemporary newspapers, published in English as well as in the different Indian languages, which tracked Mahatma Gandhi’s movements and reported on his activities, and also represented what ordinary Indians thought of him.

The text explains that (1) How do you think the nature of the source affects what is being said in these reports?

The text explains that why are newspapers an important source for the study of the national movement?

The text explains that how are these sources different from official accounts?

The text explains that what do private letters and autobiographies tell us about an individual?

The text explains that read any two autobiographies of nationalist leaders.

The text explains that look at the different ways in which the authors represent their own life and times, and interpret the national movement.

The text explains that choose any event that took place during the national movement.

The text explains that try and read the letters and speeches of the leaders of the time.

The text explains that he could be a local leader from the region where you live.

The text explains that try and see how the local leaders viewed the activities of the national leadership at the top.

The text explains that write about the movement based on your reading.

The text explains that on a map of Gujarat plot the line of the march and mark the major towns and villages that it passed along the route.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 3: Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement
